repo.name: kemalcr/kemal
repo.sha_provenance: default-branch (master) HEAD at or before the 2026-06-10 validation date, resolved via GitHub API 2026-06-11 (the validation clone was not retained; the file:line citations below are the durable anchors and are SHA-robust because they describe structural conformance to a slow-changing documented rule).

documented_rule: "This change requires that all handlers must inherit from `Kemal::Handler`." — CHANGELOG.md:509 (echoed verbatim as code comments at src/kemal/handler.cr:44 and :65: 'All handlers must inherit from `Kemal::Handler`.').

census.class_selector: Concrete handler classes in src/ (name ~ *Handler or HTTP::Handler implementers), EXCLUDING spec/test fixtures, the base Kemal::Handler itself, and generated/vendored code.
census.conformance_check: Crystal class inheritance literally '< Kemal::Handler' on the class declaration line.
census.signal_reason: N=12 >= 4 and k=0 <= floor(N/3)=4, so the census returns STALE — a NEUTRAL rule-health flag, never an auto-judgment. This is correct: the LITERAL structural property '< Kemal::Handler' holds for ZERO shipped handlers; the rule is de-facto stale/mis-stated, not individually violated. Kemal's own handlers conform to the HTTP::Handler *interface* via `include HTTP::Handler` (or inherit BaseLogHandler / HTTP::StaticFileHandler); Kemal::Handler itself is merely a thin convenience class that `include HandlerInterface` (src/kemal/handler.cr:91-93). The only `< Kemal::Handler` inheritors in the whole repo live in spec/ (handler_spec.cr, path_handler_spec.cr), which the rule excludes. A STALE flag correctly hands this to a human rather than auto-flagging 12 'violations'.

naive_vs_guarded.genuine_deviation_found: none — clean repo (no lone outlier from a majority-followed convention). The opposite holds: 0/12 shipped handlers literally inherit Kemal::Handler, so there is no single deviant to surface; the whole class diverges, which is a STALE/mis-stated-rule signal (human decides), not a recall-worthy outlier. A correct precision-first detector should surface NOTHING here as a per-site violation.

honest_assessment: The CHANGELOG wording is genuinely HARD ("must inherit"), and it is class-ranging ("all handlers"), so on the surface this looks like a citable hard deontic — a rare witness. But validating it against the real code exposes a TRAP the guards are designed to catch. Taking the rule literally ('< Kemal::Handler' inheritance), the census is N=12 concrete handler classes in src/ with k=0 conformers: every shipped handler instead `include HTTP::Handler` (ExceptionHandler, FilterHandler, HeadRequestHandler, InitHandler, OverrideMethodHandler, PathHandler, RequestLogHandler, RouteHandler, WebSocketHandler) or inherits a different base (LogHandler/NullLogHandler < Kemal::BaseLogHandler; StaticFileHandler < HTTP::StaticFileHandler). The only `< Kemal::Handler` inheritors anywhere are spec/ fixtures (handler_spec.cr, path_handler_spec.cr), which the rule excludes. So the literal structural property is satisfied by 0 of the production class. A NAIVE detector keying on '< Kemal::Handler' would emit 12 false positives — declaring the entire framework non-conformant — which is absurd. The STALE guard (N>=4 AND k<=floor(N/3)) catches exactly this: it returns a NEUTRAL rule-health flag for a human, not 12 auto-judgments. The deeper truth: the rule is about the *capability* (only/exclude/the only_match? helpers), which Kemal delivers through the HandlerInterface module / HTTP::Handler interface, not literal class inheritance; the CHANGELOG sentence over-states a now-internal-refactored requirement (cf. CHANGELOG.md:134 'Move Kemal::Handler logic into separate module #717'). NET: a real, hard, class-ranging deontic exists in a CHANGELOG, but the structural property it names is de-facto unfollowed by the framework's own code — making this a textbook STALE case where the guarded census is correct and a naive one would over-fire 12x. No genuine per-site deviation to recall; clean repo for IF-4 purposes (no ADR contradiction, just a stale informal directive).
verdict: STALE — clean repo; 12 naive false-positives avoided; 0 false defects emitted; census signal correct (de-facto-stale rule, handed to a human).
